The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly observations show total assets oscillating in a narrow band (~4.477T to ~4.502T) with no clear trend, consistent with a full reinvestment policy (not active QT or QE). The 4-week change is -4,238M and 13-week change is -6,990M, both modest relative to the ~4.49T level (about -0.16% over 13 weeks), and the most recent print (4,482,349) is close to the 13-week average (4,487,402), suggesting mean-reverting noise rather than a sustained runoff or expansion. The policy language explicitly describes reinvestment of principal and rollover of maturing Treasuries at auction—this is balance-sheet-neutral language, not an announced runoff cap or QE program, which matches the flat/no-trend measurement pattern. My prior view (if it called runoff) should be reassessed: the trend is not a clean decline but a choppy flat pattern with a slight downward drift. Given the policy is explicitly reinvestment (holding levels steady), and the recent 4w/13w changes are small and within the noise band seen over the whole 13-week window, I read this as essentially flat with a slight downward lean.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-week consecutive decline exceeding the 13w average by a wide margin, or explicit policy language announcing a runoff cap/QT program, would confirm sustained contraction.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been range-bound near the low end of its 12-month band (1.15-1.77pp), currently at 1.18pp, only 0.03pp above the 12-month low and at just 5% of the range (slope_range_position 0.05). The last ten readings oscillate narrowly between 1.15 and 1.19 with no clear trend, and the most recent print (1.18) matches the prior value shown, so my last call (essentially flat) played out correctly. Medium-term momentum is mildly flattening: slope_change_63d is -0.22pp, though the shorter slope_change_21d (-0.05) and slope_change_252d (-0.05) show the flattening has largely stalled recently, consistent with the flat 10-observation pattern. Policy language shows the Fed moved from actually hiking (raising the funds rate) to holding steady, removing the explicit hike language - a pause tends to reduce near-term upward pressure on the front end, which is mildly supportive of stabilization rather than renewed flattening. With the spread pinned near cycle lows but not making new lows, and no fresh catalyst evident in the language changes, I expect the spread to stay near current levels into month-end rather than move decisively in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.15 (new 12-month low) or above 1.25 would indicate a decisive flattening or steepening move, respectively.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, consistent monotonic rise over the last 13 observations, from -0.47 to -0.28. This is a steady tightening trend: 4-week change is +0.07 and 13-week change is +0.17, both positive and of similar per-period pace, indicating no deceleration. The index sits at its 52-week high (range_position = 1.00), meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the observed window, though still well below the zero long-run average (distance_from_zero = -0.28), so conditions remain accommodative in absolute terms even as they tighten at the margin. My prior view (if it called continued easing) would have been wrong given this data — the trend has clearly reversed from loose/easing toward tightening over the recent stretch, and there's no sign in the last few points of the tightening stalling; the last three deltas (-0.31→-0.30→-0.28) are still positive and roughly steady. The new policy language flags close monitoring of global developments and balance of risks, which is consistent with an environment where the Fed is watching financial conditions tighten, but doesn't itself signal a shift. Given the unbroken multi-week uptrend and position at the top of its recent range, I expect continuation of modest tightening into next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A reversal to flat/declining weekly NFCI prints for 2-3 consecutive weeks, or a sharp drop back toward the 13-week average, would negate this tightening call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (1.41) ticked down from 1.42 but sits well above the recent trough of 1.28 and mid-way in the 12-month range (range_position 0.20 from bottom, meaning still closer to the low). Looking at the last several prints, breakeven rose from 1.28 to a peak of 1.42 then eased slightly to 1.41 — essentially a plateau after a rebound. The 21-day and 63-day changes are still negative (-0.12, -0.07), reflecting the earlier decline baked into the window, but the most recent four readings (1.34, 1.36, 1.40, 1.42, 1.41) show a modest uptrend that has now flattened rather than reversed. Policy language has shifted more dovish/cautious — "declined further" and survey measures "little changed" — which is a lagging characterization likely referencing the earlier drop, not the recent stabilization. My prior view (if it called continued decline) was too pessimistic given the actual bounce off the 1.28 low. Given the flattening at 1.41-1.42 after a recovery, and no fresh measurement showing renewed deterioration, I read this as a pause rather than a resumption of the downtrend. Distance from the 2% target remains large (-0.59pp), keeping any upside bias muted.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.35 or a fresh multi-week decline would confirm renewed downtrend; a push above 1.45 would confirm reflation resuming.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI jumped to 1.24% y/y from 0.64%, a +0.60pp surge, and my prior "up" call was correct in direction (though the magnitude was larger than typical drift). However, the underlying mom print for that latest observation was -0.05%, meaning the big yoy jump was almost entirely base effects (a very negative print rolling out of the 12-month window) rather than fresh momentum. The 3-month annualized headline is actually -0.14%, and the mom 3-month average is slightly negative (-0.01%), indicating flat-to-soft actual monthly momentum despite the yoy print's sharp rise. Core PCE remains essentially pinned near 1.19-1.20% y/y, still 0.81pp below target, with a slightly negative 3m yoy change — no acceleration signal there. Policy language confirms this is being read as transitory (energy/import price effects dissipating), consistent with base-effect-driven yoy moves rather than a genuine turn in trend inflation. Given the outgoing mom (0.25%) is larger than the recent 3-month average pace, another old high print will roll out, which could produce a further mechanical yoy pop, but the underlying trend is flat-to-tepid. I lean toward another modest headline yoy increase next month on base effects, but with lower conviction than the move itself might suggest, since core shows no real acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A core PCE print breaking above 1.3% y/y or headline mom turning solidly positive (>0.2%) for two consecutive months would confirm genuine acceleration rather than base effects.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate just fell to 4.80%, a fresh 12-month low, down 0.20pp over 3 months, 0.40pp over 6 months, and 0.90pp over the trailing year. There is no Sahm-rule style acceleration in the rise off the low—there is no rise at all; unrate_above_12m_low is 0.00pp and range_position is at the bottom of the 12-month band. The trend has been a steady, orderly grind lower with no signs of inflection. Policy language reinforces this: the updated commentary describes "ongoing job gains and declining unemployment" and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions," a clear upgrade from the prior statement's more cautious "held steady" language. This is consistent tightening, not loosening. The level (4.80%) is now essentially at or slightly above many estimates of the natural rate, though the distance_from_natural metric here (0.80pp) suggests further room to tighten before hitting typical full-employment thresholds. Momentum, policy language, and the absence of any reversal signal all point the same direction. Absent a shock, the most likely near-term outcome is either continued modest decline or a flat print near the same level, given how compressed the recent range has become and the possibility of a pause after a large one-month drop.
Would change its mind if: A jump in unemployment rate of 0.2pp+ in the next report, or policy language reverting to describing labor market weakening, would reverse this view.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has declined from 2.07 to 1.94 over the last 10 readings, with the 21-day change at -0.38pp — a sharp recent drop despite the 63-day change being milder (-0.11pp) and the 252-day change still positive (+0.21pp). This suggests the longer-term uptrend has stalled and reversed sharply in the most recent month. The yield sits at only 0.32 of its 12-month range, closer to the low (1.68) than the high (2.50), indicating the long end is drifting toward the lower part of its range. The 30s10s slope remains a healthy 0.81pp, showing no acute long-end-specific distress or steepening pressure at the very long end; the curve context is fairly stable. Policy language changed to note inflation compensation "declined further," which historically coincides with lower nominal long-end yields as embedded inflation/term premium compresses. Combined with the sharp downward move in yields and the low range position, the evidence points to continued or persistent softness in the term premium proxy rather than a rebound. Given the strength and recency of the -0.38pp move and the corroborating survey language, I expect the 10-year yield to be modestly lower or flat at month-end, leaning down.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above ~2.05 in dgs10_level within the next few readings, or a reversal of the 21-day decline trend, would falsify this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Treasury runoff/reinvestment changes would directly affect long-end supply/demand and term premium.
  term_premium lacks inflation_expectations — Further confirmation of inflation compensation trends would validate the drift lower in nominal yields.

I lean down but with modest conviction given the deceleration in the last two data points.
